does: This paper identifies and demonstrates a critical security vulnerability in the function calling capabilities of LLMs, introducing a novel 'jailbreak function' attack that exploits alignment discrepancies and insufficient safety filtering in function call processing. The authors conduct empirical evaluations across six state-of-the-art models including GPT-4o, Claude-3.5-Sonnet, and Gemini-1.5-Pro, achieving over 90% average attack success rates. The work provides detailed analysis of why function calls are particularly vulnerable to jailbreaking compared to standard chat interfaces, examining how user coercion and the absence of rigorous safety filters in function processing pathways enable these attacks. The paper also proposes and evaluates defensive strategies, including defensive prompting approaches to mitigate these vulnerabilities.
connects: This work extends existing research on LLM safety and adversarial robustness, which has primarily focused on chat-based interactions and prompt injection attacks. It builds on established vulnerability research in LLMs while identifying a previously overlooked attack surface in function calling—a feature increasingly integral to LLM deployment but under-studied from a security perspective. The paper connects to prior work on alignment failures and safety filter effectiveness, while introducing function-specific failure modes that differ from standard jailbreak techniques. It also relates to broader research on API security and the challenges of maintaining consistent safety guarantees across different interface modalities and operational modes of language models.
enables: This research establishes function calling as a critical safety consideration for future LLM development and deployment. It enables the design and implementation of more robust safety mechanisms specifically tailored to function calling contexts, including improved safety filters and validation procedures for function invocation. The work opens new directions for studying how safety properties vary across different LLM interface types and operational modes, potentially revealing similar vulnerabilities in other auxiliary features. It supports the development of better evaluation frameworks and benchmarks for function calling security, while motivating research into alignment techniques that maintain robustness across diverse interaction modalities. The findings also drive adoption of defensive practices in both LLM development and downstream applications relying on function calling capabilities.
